defenses: Invalidity — anticipation §102, obviousness §103 (Graham/KSR), or §112 indefiniteness/written description/enablement (35 U.S.C. §282(b) defense; invalidity must be proven by clear and convincing evidence under Microsoft v. i4i)
Patent-ineligible subject matter under §101 (Alice/Mayo two-step abstract-idea/law-of-nature framework)
Non-infringement (claim does not read on accused product literally or under equivalents; prosecution history estoppel under Festo)
Inequitable conduct / unenforceability (Therasense but-for materiality + specific intent to deceive PTO)
§286 six-year damages limitation; §287 marking/notice limit on damages; license, patent exhaustion (Quanta/Impression Products), or experimental-use/prior-commercial-use (§273) defense
causes.basis: 35 U.S.C. §271; §282; §286; Markman v. Westview Instruments, Inc., 517 U.S. 370 (1996); Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank Int'l, 573 U.S. 208 (2014); KSR Int'l Co. v. Teleflex Inc., 550 U.S. 398 (2007); Halo Electronics, Inc. v. Pulse Electronics, Inc., 579 U.S. 93 (2016); SCA Hygiene Products Aktiebolag v. First Quality Baby Products, LLC, 580 U.S. 328 (2017) (no laches defense against damages within the §286 period)

causes.n: Lanham Act §32 Trademark Infringement
causes.sol_years: none (no federal statute of limitations; laches gauged by analogous state SOL)
elements: Plaintiff owns a valid, federally registered trademark (15 U.S.C. §1057(b) — registration is prima facie evidence of validity, ownership, and the exclusive right to use the mark in commerce); mark is distinctive (inherently or via secondary meaning) and not generic
Defendant used in commerce, without consent, a reproduction/counterfeit/copy/colorable imitation of the registered mark
Use in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services

Likelihood of confusion as to source, sponsorship, or affiliation (multi-factor test — e.g., Polaroid/Sleekcraft/DuPont factors: strength of mark, similarity of marks, proximity of goods, defendant's intent, actual confusion, etc.)
defenses: No likelihood of confusion (multi-factor analysis fails)
Mark invalidity — genericness, mere descriptiveness without secondary meaning, abandonment, functionality, or fraud on the PTO
Fair use / nominative fair use; First Amendment / expressive use (after Jack Daniel's, the Rogers v. Grimaldi threshold test does not apply when the mark is used as a source identifier)
Laches and acquiescence (no statutory SOL; analogous state limitations period as a guide / presumption)
Lack of use in commerce; prior use / senior user or limited-area defense (§1115(b)); absence of standing or trademark ownership
causes.basis: 15 U.S.C. §1114; 15 U.S.C. §1057(b); Two Pesos, Inc. v. Taco Cabana, Inc., 505 U.S. 763 (1992); KP Permanent Make-Up, Inc. v. Lasting Impression I, Inc., 543 U.S. 111 (2004); Jack Daniel's Properties, Inc. v. VIP Products LLC, 599 U.S. 140 (2023); Polaroid Corp. v. Polarad Electronics Corp., 287 F.2d 492 (2d Cir. 1961)

causes.n: Lanham Act §43(a) False Advertising / Trade Dress
causes.sol_years: none (no federal statute of limitations; laches gauged by analogous state SOL)
elements: A false or misleading statement of fact in commercial advertising or promotion (false advertising), OR a protectable trade dress / unregistered mark used in a manner likely to cause confusion (15 U.S.C. §1125(a))

For false advertising: the statement was literally false or, though literally true, likely to mislead/confuse; the statement actually deceived or has a tendency to deceive a substantial segment of the audience; the deception is material (likely to influence purchasing decisions); the goods/services entered interstate commerce; and plaintiff has been or is likely to be injured

For trade dress: the trade dress is distinctive (inherently, or — for product design and color — only via secondary meaning), is non-functional (plaintiff bears the burden of proving non-functionality, 15 U.S.C. §1125(a)(3)), and defendant's use causes a likelihood of confusion
Plaintiff falls within the Lanham Act's zone of interests and shows commercial/reputational injury proximately caused by the deception (Lexmark statutory-standing analysis)
defenses: Statement is non-actionable puffery, true, or adequately substantiated (no falsity / no material deception)
Trade dress is functional (utilitarian functionality under TrafFix, or aesthetic functionality) or lacks secondary meaning (Wal-Mart product-design rule requires secondary meaning)
Lack of Lanham Act statutory standing — outside the zone of interests or no proximate cause (Lexmark)
Laches / acquiescence (analogous state SOL); no use in commerce; no likelihood of confusion
First Amendment / non-commercial-speech protection
causes.basis: 15 U.S.C. §1125(a); Lexmark Int'l, Inc. v. Static Control Components, Inc., 572 U.S. 118 (2014); Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. v. Samara Bros., 529 U.S. 205 (2000); TrafFix Devices, Inc. v. Marketing Displays, Inc., 532 U.S. 23 (2001); Two Pesos, Inc. v. Taco Cabana, Inc., 505 U.S. 763 (1992)

causes.n: Defend Trade Secrets Act (18 U.S.C. §1836)
elements: Existence of a trade secret — information (financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering, including patterns, plans, compilations, programs, devices, formulas, methods, techniques, or processes) that derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use (18 U.S.C. §1839(3)(B))
Owner took reasonable measures under the circumstances to keep the information secret (18 U.S.C. §1839(3)(A))
The trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce (federal jurisdictional nexus, 18 U.S.C. §1836(b)(1))

Misappropriation — acquisition by improper means, or disclosure/use without consent by one who used improper means to acquire it or who knew or had reason to know it was derived from a person who acquired it by improper means or owed a duty to maintain its secrecy (18 U.S.C. §1839(5)–(6))
Resulting damages, unjust enrichment, or threatened misappropriation warranting injunctive relief
defenses: Information is not a trade secret — generally known, readily ascertainable by proper means, or lacks independent economic value
Owner failed to take reasonable secrecy measures (18 U.S.C. §1839(3)(A))
Proper acquisition — independent development, reverse engineering, or other lawful/proper means (no improper means, hence no misappropriation; §1839(6)(B))

18 U.S.C. §1836(d) three-year statute of limitations (accrues on discovery or when misappropriation should have been discovered by reasonable diligence; a continuing misappropriation is a single claim)

DTSA whistleblower immunity (§1833(b)); no improper means; failure to identify the trade secret with sufficient particularity (Twombly/Iqbal plausibility); note DTSA does not preempt state trade-secret law
causes.basis: 18 U.S.C. §1836(b); §1839; §1836(d) (3-year SOL); Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, Pub. L. No. 114-153, 130 Stat. 376; see, e.g., Attia v. Google LLC, 983 F.3d 420 (9th Cir. 2020) [VERIFY]

causes.n: False Claims Act — Qui Tam (31 U.S.C. §§3729-3730)
elements: Defendant presented, or caused to be presented, a claim for payment or approval to the United States (or made/used a false record or statement material to such a claim, or to an obligation to pay money to the Government — reverse false claim)
The claim or statement was false or fraudulent
Falsity was material to the Government's payment decision (Escobar materiality — natural tendency to influence, not minor/insubstantial)
Defendant acted with scienter: actual knowledge, deliberate ignorance, or reckless disregard of truth/falsity (no specific intent to defraud required)
The false claim caused the United States to pay money or forgo money owed (damages — trebled — plus per-claim civil penalties)

For relator standing/recovery: action filed under seal, served on the Government with a written disclosure of material evidence (§3730(b)(2)); first-to-file and public-disclosure bars not triggered (or relator is an original source)
defenses: Rule 9(b) particularity for the fraud claim (who/what/when/where/how; reliable indicia an actual false claim was submitted)
scienter/materiality (Escobar materiality; Government's continued payment with knowledge defeats materiality; Safeco objective-reasonableness on knowledge per SuperValu)
§3730(e) public-disclosure bar / first-to-file bar / Government-action bar; relator not an original source
lack of subject-matter jurisdiction / Article III standing (and Government dismissal authority under §3730(c)(2)(A) per Polansky)
§3731(b) statute of limitations (6 years from violation / 3 years from Government knowledge, capped at 10 years)
causes.basis: 31 U.S.C. §§3729-3730; Universal Health Servs., Inc. v. United States ex rel. Escobar, 579 U.S. 176 (2016); United States ex rel. Schutte v. SuperValu Inc., 598 U.S. 739 (2023); United States ex rel. Polansky v. Exec. Health Res., Inc., 599 U.S. 419 (2023)

causes.n: Tucker Act — Contract Claim Against the United States (28 U.S.C. §1491)
elements: Plaintiff asserts a claim against the United States founded upon an express or implied-in-fact contract with the Government, the Constitution, a money-mandating statute, or a regulation (the Tucker Act waives sovereign immunity but creates no substantive right — a separate money-mandating source is required)
Existence of a contract: mutual intent to contract, offer and acceptance, consideration, and a Government representative with actual authority to bind the United States
Plaintiff performed its obligations (or tendered/was excused from performance)
The United States breached a contractual or other money-mandating duty
The breach caused damages, and the claim sounds in money (Court of Federal Claims jurisdiction; over $10,000 exclusive of district-court 'Little Tucker Act' concurrent jurisdiction up to $10,000)
defenses: lack of subject-matter jurisdiction (no money-mandating source; claim sounds in tort, not contract; sovereign immunity not waived for the asserted right)
lack of actual authority of the Government agent to bind the United States (no implied/apparent-authority estoppel against the Government)
28 U.S.C. §2501 six-year statute of limitations (jurisdictional, not subject to equitable tolling per John R. Sand)
failure to exhaust required contractual/CDA dispute procedures (Contract Disputes Act, 41 U.S.C. §7101 et seq., where applicable)
Twombly/Iqbal plausibility (no plausible allegation of an enforceable contract or breach)
causes.basis: 28 U.S.C. §1491 (and §1346(a)(2) Little Tucker Act); United States v. Mitchell, 463 U.S. 206 (1983); John R. Sand & Gravel Co. v. United States, 552 U.S. 130 (2008)

causes.n: Federal Tort Claims Act (28 U.S.C. §§1346(b), 2671-2680)
causes.sol_years: 2/0.5 (admin claim 2 yrs; suit 6 mo. after denial)
elements: The claim is for injury or loss of property, or personal injury or death
Caused by the negligent or wrongful act or omission of a federal employee (not an independent contractor) acting within the scope of office or employment

Under circumstances where the United States, if a private person, would be liable to the claimant in accordance with the law of the place where the act or omission occurred (state substantive tort law supplies the cause of action)

Jurisdictional exhaustion: claimant presented the claim in writing to the appropriate federal agency for a sum certain within 2 years, and the agency finally denied it (or 6 months elapsed) before suit (§2675)
No enumerated §2680 exception applies (notably no discretionary-function, intentional-tort, or other bar)
defenses: lack of subject-matter jurisdiction (failure to exhaust the §2675 administrative claim; sum-certain defect — jurisdictional)
§2680 exceptions — discretionary-function exception (Berkovitz/Gaubert two-step), intentional-tort, misrepresentation, and other enumerated bars
Feres doctrine (no liability for service-members' injuries incident to military service); independent-contractor exclusion
§2401(b) statute of limitations (2-year administrative-claim bar / 6-month post-denial suit bar; non-jurisdictional and equitably tollable per Wong)
no private-person analog liability under the law of the place; substantive state-law tort defenses
causes.basis: 28 U.S.C. §§1346(b), 2671-2680; United States v. Gaubert, 499 U.S. 315 (1991); United States v. Wong, 575 U.S. 402 (2015); Feres v. United States, 340 U.S. 135 (1950)

causes.n: APA §706 Judicial Review of Agency Action (5 U.S.C. §§701-706)
elements: Plaintiff suffered legal wrong because of, or is adversely affected/aggrieved by, final agency action (or agency action unlawfully withheld/unreasonably delayed)
The challenged action is 'final agency action' (consummation of decisionmaking from which rights/obligations flow per Bennett v. Spear) for which there is no other adequate remedy in a court (§704)

Plaintiff falls within the zone of interests protected by the statute and has Article III standing; the action is reviewable (not committed to agency discretion by law and not statutorily precluded, §701(a))
Plaintiff has exhausted required administrative remedies / the claim is ripe

The agency action is arbitrary, capricious, an abuse of discretion, otherwise not in accordance with law, contrary to constitutional right, in excess of statutory authority, or without observance of procedure required by law (§706(2)) — reviewed on the administrative record
defenses: lack of subject-matter jurisdiction / no final agency action (§704) / action committed to agency discretion or statutorily precluded (§701(a))
Article III standing (injury-in-fact, causation, redressability) and prudential zone-of-interests / ripeness / mootness
failure to exhaust administrative remedies
deferential standard of review (arbitrary-and-capricious / Loper Bright de novo on questions of law; record-review limits the challenge)
28 U.S.C. §2401(a) six-year default statute of limitations (accrues when the right of action first accrues per Corner Post)
causes.basis: 5 U.S.C. §§701-706; Bennett v. Spear, 520 U.S. 154 (1997); Motor Vehicle Mfrs. Ass'n v. State Farm, 463 U.S. 29 (1983); Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024); Corner Post, Inc. v. Bd. of Governors of the FRS, 603 U.S. 799 (2024)

causes.n: 28 U.S.C. §2255 Motion to Vacate Sentence / Federal Habeas (28 U.S.C. §2254)
elements: Movant is a federal prisoner in custody under sentence of a federal court (for §2254, a person in custody pursuant to a state-court judgment)

A ground for relief: the sentence was imposed in violation of the Constitution or laws of the United States; the court lacked jurisdiction; the sentence exceeded the maximum authorized by law; or the sentence/conviction is otherwise subject to collateral attack
For non-defaulted constitutional claims (e.g., ineffective assistance under Strickland): deficient performance plus prejudice (reasonable probability of a different result)

Procedural prerequisites: claim not procedurally defaulted (or cause-and-prejudice / actual-innocence excuse shown); not a successive petition absent court-of-appeals authorization (§2255(h)); filed within the 1-year limitations period (§2255(f))

For §2254: exhaustion of available state remedies and, on adjudicated claims, the AEDPA §2254(d) showing that the state decision was contrary to/unreasonable application of clearly established Supreme Court law or based on an unreasonable determination of fact
defenses: §2255(f) / §2244(d) one-year statute of limitations (no equitable tolling absent diligence + extraordinary circumstance per Holland)
procedural default (cause and prejudice or actual-innocence gateway required) / failure to exhaust state remedies (§2254)
§2255(h) / §2244(b) bar on second-or-successive petitions absent court-of-appeals certification
AEDPA §2254(d) deference (Harrington v. Richter — no fairminded jurist could disagree) and Teague non-retroactivity
lack of subject-matter jurisdiction / no cognizable constitutional or jurisdictional defect (non-constitutional, non-jurisdictional errors generally not cognizable absent a fundamental defect)
causes.basis: 28 U.S.C. §2255 (and §2254 / §2244 AEDPA); Strickland v. Washington, 466 U.S. 668 (1984); Harrington v. Richter, 562 U.S. 86 (2011); Holland v. Florida, 560 U.S. 631 (2010)

causes.n: ERISA § 502(a)(1)(B) — Recovery of Plan Benefits Due
causes.sol_years: varies by analogous state contract SOL (commonly 4-6); plan may set shorter contractual limit (enforceable per Heimeshoff)
elements: Plaintiff is a participant or beneficiary of an ERISA-governed employee benefit plan (29 U.S.C. § 1132(a)(1)(B))
An ERISA plan exists and the claim arises under its terms (not a payroll practice or other ERISA-exempt arrangement)
Plaintiff was entitled to a benefit under the express terms of the plan
Plaintiff exhausted the plan's internal administrative claims-and-appeals procedures (or exhaustion is excused/futile)
The plan administrator or fiduciary denied, reduced, or failed to pay the benefit due, or failed to enforce/clarify rights under the plan terms

Where the plan grants the administrator discretionary authority, the denial was arbitrary and capricious (abuse of discretion); absent such a grant, the denial was wrong under de novo review (Firestone)
defenses: Failure to exhaust ERISA plan administrative remedies (administrative-exhaustion bar)
Plan-imposed contractual limitations period bars suit (Heimeshoff)
Twombly/Iqbal plausibility — complaint fails to identify the specific plan terms entitling plaintiff to benefits
Arbitrary-and-capricious / abuse-of-discretion deference where plan confers discretionary authority on administrator (Firestone); structural conflict weighed as a factor (Glenn)
Lack of statutory standing — plaintiff is not a 'participant' or 'beneficiary'
causes.basis: 29 U.S.C. § 1132(a)(1)(B); Firestone Tire & Rubber Co. v. Bruch, 489 U.S. 101 (1989); Heimeshoff v. Hartford Life & Accident Ins. Co., 571 U.S. 99 (2013); Metropolitan Life Ins. Co. v. Glenn, 554 U.S. 105 (2008)

causes.n: ERISA § 502(a)(3) — Breach of Fiduciary Duty / Other Equitable Relief
causes.sol_years: 6 years from the breach/violation, OR 3 years from the date plaintiff had actual knowledge of the breach if that occurs first (29 U.S.C. § 1113); 6-year limit extended to 6 years from discovery in cases of fraud or concealment
elements: Defendant is a fiduciary — i.e., exercises discretionary authority/control over plan management, administration, or assets (29 U.S.C. § 1002(21)(A))

Defendant breached an ERISA fiduciary duty: loyalty (solely in participants'/beneficiaries' interest), prudence (prudent-expert standard), diversification, or adherence to plan documents (29 U.S.C. § 1104(a)), or engaged in a prohibited transaction (§ 1106)

Plaintiff (participant, beneficiary, or fiduciary) seeks 'appropriate equitable relief' — e.g., surcharge, equitable estoppel, reformation, injunction, restitution (equitable, against identifiable property), constructive trust, disgorgement of profits
The relief sought is typically equitable in nature, not legal damages (Mertens; Great-West Life v. Knudson)
The § 502(a)(3) 'catchall' claim is appropriate because § 502(a)(1)(B) or another subsection does not provide adequate relief (Varity)
Causal connection between the breach/violation and the loss or unjust enrichment to be remedied
defenses: Adequate relief available under § 502(a)(1)(B) bars duplicative 'catchall' equitable claim (Varity Corp. v. Howe)
Relief sought is legal (money damages) rather than 'appropriate equitable relief,' and is therefore unavailable (Mertens; Great-West Life v. Knudson)
ERISA § 1113 statute of limitations/repose — 6 years from breach or 3 years from actual knowledge, whichever is earlier (no actionable claim outside the window)
Article III standing — no concrete particularized injury or loss to the plan/participant, particularly in a defined-benefit plan (Thole v. U.S. Bank)
Twombly/Iqbal plausibility — failure to plead specific imprudent conduct rather than merely a poor investment outcome (Hughes v. Northwestern Univ.)
Defendant is not an ERISA fiduciary, or is a non-fiduciary who cannot be liable for 'knowing participation' in monetary terms under § 502(a)(3) (Mertens)
causes.basis: 29 U.S.C. § 1132(a)(3); 29 U.S.C. §§ 1104, 1106, 1109, 1113; Mertens v. Hewitt Associates, 508 U.S. 248 (1993); Varity Corp. v. Howe, 516 U.S. 489 (1996); Great-West Life & Annuity Ins. Co. v. Knudson, 534 U.S. 204 (2002); CIGNA Corp. v. Amara, 563 U.S. 421 (2011); Hughes v. Northwestern Univ., 595 U.S. 170 (2022); Thole v. U.S. Bank N.A., 590 U.S. 538 (2020)
